AI SME Policy Navigator

Data sources

This navigator combines 399 records from the OECD.AI Policy Observatory with 107 additional records identified through a parallel desktop review of G7 AI policy landscapes (Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom, United States, and EU-level initiatives).

OECD.AI records carry the original classification, metadata, and taxonomy from that platform. Additional records follow the same schema and were reviewed for consistency.

Additional records are highlighted with a light blue row background. Use the Source filter to view each dataset independently.

SME classification

The SME Relevance column indicates whether and how a policy targets SMEs:

  • Explicit — formally designated for SMEs or micro-enterprises (stated in the title, eligibility criteria, or official documentation).
  • Inferred — not formally restricted to SMEs but functionally relevant by design, funding mechanism, or sectoral focus (e.g. open R&D calls, general-purpose tax credits, skills programmes).

Archetype labels are assigned using a keyword and policy-type logic consistent across both datasets. Multiple labels appear when a policy spans several stages; the primary label reflects the most central stage. Governance bodies, infrastructure investments, and non-AI-specific programmes receive N/A regardless of SME relevance.

For OECD.AI records, SME relevance was determined by the OECD.AI editorial team. Archetype labels for additional records are illustrative classifications — not official OECD designations.

SME AI Adoption Archetypes

The SME Archetype column classifies each policy by the AI adoption maturity stage it most concretely supports, based on the OECD analysis of SME AI adoption trajectories:

AI Novice Firms just starting out on their AI journey. AI use is limited in scope or complexity — typically isolated use cases involving specific individuals or teams, relying on embedded AI features or off-the-shelf tools such as LLMs.
AI Optimiser Firms more advanced in their adoption, comfortably using a wide variety of digital tools and integrating off-the-shelf AI products across multiple departments for a broad range of use cases, with growing co-ordination among use cases.
AI Explorer Firms developing custom AI models, including training models on their own data or experimenting with agentic workflows. Typically found in data-intensive sectors. AI use remains function-specific or isolated, not yet transformative across the entire company.
AI Champion Firms leading in AI adoption, deploying AI widely across the business to support a broad range of tasks and inform operational and strategic decision-making. AI is embedded at all levels of the organisation through unified, intelligent systems.

A bold badge indicates the primary target stage; a faded badge indicates secondary relevance.

Policy tagging

Each record is tagged across four dimensions. Tags are applied only when that dimension is a primary and explicit focus of the policy.

Barriers — the SME AI adoption obstacle(s) the policy helps overcome:

  • Awareness — limited knowledge of available AI tools, solutions, or providers
  • Business case — no obvious use case for the business or sector, or unclear return on investment
  • Cost — high upfront investment or financial uncertainty associated with AI adoption
  • Skills — insufficient in-house technical skills or human capacity to adopt AI
  • Integration — difficulty integrating AI into existing systems, processes, or workflows
  • Data access & quality — difficulty accessing enough relevant, clean, or interoperable data
  • Privacy & security — concerns about data protection regulation, security requirements, or risk of personal data misuse
  • Regulatory uncertainty — uncertainty about which regulations apply, legal liability, or compliance requirements
  • Trust — lack of trust or confidence in AI outputs, decisions, or recommendations

Support types — how the policy delivers support:

  • Advisory — expert consultancy, AI readiness assessments, or tailored guidance
  • Compliance guidance — regulatory guidance, sandboxes, or legal clarity to help businesses navigate AI rules
  • Financial — grants, subsidies, tax incentives, or co-investment
  • Infrastructure — shared access to compute, cloud, or data infrastructure
  • Knowledge exchange — peer learning networks, communities of practice, or knowledge-sharing platforms
  • Tools — access to AI tools, software platforms, or technical resources
  • Training — skills development programmes, courses, or bootcamps for staff and managers

Industry sector tags identify the economic sector primarily targeted; policies with no specific sector focus appear as All sectors. Theme tags capture the cross-cutting policy domain (e.g. Digital Economy, Public Governance, Science & Technology).

SME AI Adoption Archetypes

AI Novice Awareness & first steps — basic digital tools, orientation, introductory support.
AI Optimiser Process improvement — applying AI to automate or optimise existing operations.
AI Explorer Experimentation & R&D — piloting new use cases, innovation grants, start-up stage.
AI Champion Frontier deployment — enterprise-wide AI, scale-up growth, advanced model development.
Bold badge = primary target stage  ·  Faded badge = secondary relevance
Labels follow keyword-based classification consistent with OECD.AI Observatory practice.
